title: FamilyRelationships

lean_statement.name: Pa
PaSet
lean_statement.code:
/-- `PaSet G A` -- the set of *parents* of the set of vertices
`A` in `G`: the union of the parent sets of each `v ∈ A`.
Matches the LN's set-input `\Pa^G(A)`. -/
def PaSet (G : CDMG α) (A : Set α) : Set α :=
  ⋃ v ∈ A, Pa G v

lean_statement.name: Ch
ChSet
lean_statement.code:
/-- `ChSet G A` -- the set of *children* of the set of vertices
`A` in `G`. Matches the LN's set-input `\Ch^G(A)`. -/
def ChSet (G : CDMG α) (A : Set α) : Set α :=
  ⋃ v ∈ A, Ch G v

lean_statement.name: Sib
Anc
self_mem_Anc
lean_statement.code:
/-- LN "Note: `v ∈ Anc^G(v)`" (def 3.5, item 4). The witness is
the trivial walk `Walk.nil v`. The `v ∈ G` hypothesis is the
LN's implicit precondition (its preamble fixes `v ∈ V ⊆ G`). -/
theorem self_mem_Anc {G : CDMG α} {v : α} (hv : v ∈ G) :
    v ∈ Anc G v :=

lean_statement.name: AncSet
lean_statement.code:
/-- `AncSet G A` -- the set of *ancestors* of the set `A` in `G`.
Matches the LN's set-input `\Anc^G(A)`. -/
def AncSet (G : CDMG α) (A : Set α) : Set α :=
  ⋃ v ∈ A, Anc G v

lean_statement.name: subset_Anc_set
lean_statement.code:
/-- LN "Note: `A ⊆ Anc^G(A)`" (def 3.5, item 4). Each
`v ∈ A ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` is its own ancestor via the trivial walk. -/
theorem subset_Anc_set {G : CDMG α} {A : Set α}
    (hA : A ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) : A ⊆ AncSet G A := by

lean_statement.name: Desc
self_mem_Desc
lean_statement.code:
/-- LN "Note: `v ∈ Desc^G(v)`" (def 3.5, item 5). Witness:
trivial walk. -/
theorem self_mem_Desc {G : CDMG α} {v : α} (hv : v ∈ G) :
    v ∈ Desc G v :=

lean_statement.name: DescSet
lean_statement.code:
/-- `DescSet G A` -- the set of *descendants* of the set `A` in
`G`. Matches the LN's set-input `\Desc^G(A)`. -/
def DescSet (G : CDMG α) (A : Set α) : Set α :=
  ⋃ v ∈ A, Desc G v

lean_statement.name: subset_Desc_set
NonDesc
lean_statement.code:
/-- `NonDesc G A` -- the set of *non-descendants* of the set `A`
in `G`: vertices of `G` (i.e. members of `G.J ∪ G.V`) that are
not descendants of any vertex in `A`. Matches the LN's
`\NonDesc^G(A)`. The LN provides no vertex-input variant. -/
def NonDesc (G : CDMG α) (A : Set α) : Set α :=
  (G.J ∪ G.V) \ DescSet G A

lean_statement.name: Sc
lean_statement.code:
/-- `Sc G v` -- the *strongly connected component* of `v` in `G`:
the intersection of the ancestor and descendant sets of `v`.
Matches the LN's `\Sc^G(v)`. Equivalently, the set of `w ∈ G`
that lie on a directed walk to `v` and on a directed walk from
`v`. -/
def Sc (G : CDMG α) (v : α) : Set α :=
  Anc G v ∩ Desc G v

lean_statement.name: self_mem_Sc
lean_statement.code:
/-- LN "Note: `v ∈ Sc^G(v)`" (def 3.5, item 7). Composes
`self_mem_Anc` and `self_mem_Desc`. -/
theorem self_mem_Sc {G : CDMG α} {v : α} (hv : v ∈ G) :
    v ∈ Sc G v :=

lean_statement.name: ScSet
lean_statement.code:
/-- `ScSet G A` -- the union of *strongly connected components*
of vertices in `A`. Matches the LN's set-input `\Sc^G(A)`. -/
def ScSet (G : CDMG α) (A : Set α) : Set α :=
  ⋃ v ∈ A, Sc G v

lean_statement.name: subset_Sc_set
Dist
self_mem_Dist
lean_statement.code:
/-- LN "Note: `v ∈ Dist^G(v)`" (def 3.5, item 8). Witness:
trivial walk, which is vacuously `IsBidirected`. -/
theorem self_mem_Dist {G : CDMG α} {v : α} (hv : v ∈ G) :
    v ∈ Dist G v :=

lean_statement.name: DistSet
lean_statement.code:
/-- `DistSet G A` -- the *district* of the set `A` in `G`.
Matches the LN's set-input `\Dist^G(A)`. -/
def DistSet (G : CDMG α) (A : Set α) : Set α :=
  ⋃ v ∈ A, Dist G v

lean_statement.name: subset_Dist_set

lean_explanation:
All eight family operators in this row live under a fixed ambient context: `α : Type*` is the vertex type and `G : CDMG α` is the CDMG under discussion, matching the LN's preamble "let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG". Vertex-input variants take `v : α` and produce `Set α`; set-input variants take `A : Set α` and also produce `Set α`. The LN's set-builder "$\{w \in G \mid \ldots\}$" maps to `{w | w ∈ G ∧ …}`, where the membership predicate `w ∈ G` is shorthand for `w ∈ G.J ∪ G.V` (unfolded by the `CDMG.mem_iff` simp lemma).

Three Mathlib idioms appear repeatedly. First, `⋃ v ∈ A, f G v` (Mathlib's `Set.iUnion₂` shape) is the canonical way to write "big union indexed over a set"; every set-input variant is literally `⋃ v ∈ A, <vertex variant> G v`, mirroring the LN's $\bigcup_{v \in A}$. Second, the `\` operator on `Set α` is set-theoretic difference (Mathlib `Set.diff`), used in `NonDesc G A := (G.J ∪ G.V) \ DescSet G A` to encode the LN's $(J \cup V) \setminus \Desc^G(A)$. Third, `∩` is set intersection, used in `Sc G v := Anc G v ∩ Desc G v` for the LN's $\Anc^G(v) \cap \Desc^G(v)$.

The three direct-edge operators `Pa`, `Ch`, `Sib` (items 1-3) each encode a single-edge condition: `w ⟶[G] v` for parents, `v ⟶[G] w` for children, `v ⟷[G] w` for siblings (the def_3_2 arrows for LN's $\tuh$ and $\huh$ respectively). The four directed-reachability operators `Anc`, `Desc`, `Sc`, plus the bidirected-reachability `Dist` (items 4-5, 7-8) bundle the `w ∈ G` guard with an existential `∃ π : Walk G w v, π.IsDirected` (or `IsBidirected` for `Dist`), reusing the def_3_4 `Walk` data type and the def_3_4 `WalkPredicates` predicates. `NonDesc` (item 6) is the LN's set complement, with the ambient set being the graph's `G.J ∪ G.V` (not `Set.univ`).

Each LN "Note: $v \in \Anc^G(v)$" / "$A \ins \Anc^G(A)$" reflexivity line becomes a small theorem (`self_mem_Anc`, `subset_Anc_set`, and analogues for `Desc`, `Sc`, `Dist`). The vertex-reflexivity theorems take `hv : v ∈ G` as an explicit hypothesis -- the LN's silent preamble $v \in V$ -- and discharge via the trivial walk `Walk.nil v`, which is vacuously both `IsDirected` and `IsBidirected`. The set-reflexivity theorems take `hA : A ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` and point each `v ∈ A` at itself inside the bigunion.
design_choices:
**The `w ∈ G` membership guard is baked into every set-builder, even where redundant.** The LN literally writes "$\{w \in G \mid \ldots\}$", and for the direct-edge operators (parents, children, siblings) the guard is propositionally redundant: a directed edge $w \tuh v$ already forces $w \in G$ via the structural constraint `E ⊆ (J ∪ V) × V`. We keep the guard anyway, because for the walk-based operators (ancestors, descendants, districts) the guard is genuinely load-bearing -- the trivial walk `Walk.nil w` exists for every `w : α`, so without the guard every vertex of the ambient type, including ones outside the graph, would land in `Anc G w`. Uniform shape across all eight operators is worth more than minimal-redundancy phrasing here, especially since downstream callers pattern-match against the same `w ∈ G ∧ _` shape regardless of which operator they invoke.

**Two `def`s per operator -- one vertex-input, one set-input -- except where the LN omits one.** The LN overloads $\Pa^G(\cdot)$ for both a vertex and a set argument; Lean cannot overload by argument shape, so we use the `Set` suffix to disambiguate (`Pa` versus `PaSet`). The LN provides no set-input variant of $\Sib^G$ and no vertex-input variant of $\NonDesc^G$ -- those slots are conspicuously absent. We mirror those omissions strictly: no `SibSet`, no vertex-input `NonDesc G v`. Inventing them would diverge from the LN and introduce decisions the LN never made (e.g. should $\NonDesc^G(v)$ exclude $v$ itself?). Downstream rows that genuinely need such variants can introduce them locally.

**Parents are allowed to lie in `G.J`; children are not.** A directed edge $w \tuh v$ satisfies $w \in J \cup V$ but constrains the *target* $v \in V$ -- so the parent set of an output node can contain input nodes, but the child set is always a subset of `G.V`. We do not restrict the parent set to `G.V`; this asymmetry is load-bearing for chapter 4 (CBN factorisation $P(V \mid J) = \prod_v P(v \mid \Pa^G(v))$, where the input nodes enter the factorisation precisely as parents of output nodes) and chapter 5 (do-calculus surgery on edges incident to $\Pa^G(\cdot)$).

**Bidirected edges are deliberately excluded from parents and children.** A bidirected edge $v \huh w$ makes $v$ a *sibling* of $w$, never a parent or child. The LN encodes this by writing $\tuh$ (directed-only) on the parent/child sides and $\huh$ on the sibling side; we match exactly, with `Pa` and `Ch` using `⟶[G]` (LN's $\tuh$, the directed-edge atom) and `Sib` using `⟷[G]` (LN's $\huh$, the bidirected atom). This also lines up cleanly with def_3_3's "into/out of" vocabulary: parents are read off "out of" edges from the parent's perspective, children off "out of" edges from the child's perspective, and siblings off edges with arrowheads at both endpoints.

**Ancestors and descendants are existentially quantified over the def_3_4 `Walk` data type, not a separate inductive reachability relation.** Composing on top of `Walk` immediately reuses every walk operation we already have -- length, support, append, reverse -- and matches the LN's prose phrasing "$\exists$ directed walk from $w$ to $v$". The walk's direction matches the LN: `Anc G v` is the existential over `Walk G w v` (walk starts at the candidate ancestor, ends at $v$); `Desc G v` flips it to `Walk G v w`. We do not define `Desc G v := Anc (reverse G) v` via a graph-reversal trick because the LN does not introduce graph reversal at this stage, and the direct two-`def` encoding is more readable.

**The LN's "Note:" reflexivity lines are encoded as proper theorems, not folded into the definition.** Each "Note: $v \in \Anc^G(v)$" / "$v \in \Sc^G(v)$" / "$v \in \Dist^G(v)$" line becomes a `self_mem_*` theorem; each "Note: $A \subseteq \cdot$" line becomes a `subset_*_set` theorem. The trivial witness is `Walk.nil v` (vacuously both `IsDirected` and `IsBidirected`). Each carries the LN's implicit preamble precondition (`v ∈ G` or `A ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V`) as an explicit hypothesis -- chapter 3's later rows then cite these by name rather than re-deriving the trivial-walk witness each time.

**`NonDesc G A := (G.J ∪ G.V) \ DescSet G A`, with the graph's vertex set as the ambient, not `Set.univ`.** The LN writes the complement against $(J \cup V)$, not against the ambient type $\alpha$. A vertex outside `G.J ∪ G.V` is not a non-descendant -- it simply isn't a vertex of $G$ at all. The set-input-only signature also mirrors the LN exactly: downstream do-calculus and ID-algorithm rows always reason about "non-descendants of some set", never of a single vertex, so a hypothetical `NonDesc G v` would be unused weight.

**`Sc G v := Anc G v ∩ Desc G v`, not a fresh walk-based primitive.** The LN literally writes the intersection. The strongly-connected-component reflexivity `v ∈ Sc^G(v)` then composes from `self_mem_Anc` and `self_mem_Desc`. The set-input variant is `ScSet G A := ⋃ v ∈ A, Sc G v`; the LN's parenthetical "(union of) strongly connected components" makes the bigunion semantics explicit -- $\Sc^G(A)$ is the union of each $v \in A$'s component, not the smallest single component containing $A$.

**Districts use `Walk.IsBidirected`, not a fresh bidirected-only walk type.** A district is the existential over walks all of whose edges are `bidir`, reusing the def_3_4 walk type with the def_3_4 `IsBidirected` predicate. Bidirected edges are symmetric (`G.L_symm`), so the walk-direction choice `Walk G v w` versus `Walk G w v` is propositionally indifferent, but we match the LN's literal phrasing $v \huh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh w$, starting at $v$. Districts deserve their own file (separate from `Anc`/`Desc`/`Sc`) because later chapters' graphical-separation criteria -- the ID algorithm in chapter 8, FCI completeness in chapter 16 -- treat districts as a primitive equivalence class on `V`, and the directed-reachability file should not pull in district lemmas as a transitive dependency for callers that only need ancestors.
